g.data-narration: Welcome to sampling distribution - the bridge between data and statistical inference for computer science.
g.text: The Bridge Between Data and Inference

g.data-narration: We rarely have data for entire populations. Instead we work with samples - smaller subsets of the population.
g.text: We cannot analyze entire populations (all users, all transactions, all program runtimes).
Instead, we take a sample - a manageable subset.
Question: What can we reliably infer about the population from this single sample?

g.data-narration: Understanding the difference between statistics and parameters is crucial for statistical inference.
g.text: Numerical measure of a population characteristic.
Usually unknown. Denoted by Greek letters:
Numerical measure of a sample characteristic.
Known value, varies from sample to sample:

g.data-narration: A sampling distribution is the probability distribution of a statistic computed from many samples.
g.text: Imagine taking every possible sample of size n from a population.
For each sample, calculate a statistic (like the mean x̄).
The sampling distribution = probability distribution of that statistic
across ALL possible samples. Not the distribution of sample data itself!

g.data-narration: The Central Limit Theorem states that for large samples, the sampling distribution of the mean becomes normal.
g.text: For a sufficiently large sample size n ≥ 30, the sampling distribution
of the sample mean x̄ will be approximately normally distributed

text.tspan: regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution!

g.text: This is the cornerstone that makes statistical inference possible.

g.data-narration: The CLT gives us the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean.
See how standard error decreases as sample size increases - larger samples give more precise estimates.
Let's work through a real example: analyzing sorting algorithm runtimes with the CLT.
g.text: Sorting algorithm runtime: μ = 50ms, σ = 12ms (right-skewed distribution)
Take sample of n = 36 runs. What is P(x̄ > 53ms)?

g.data-narration: The sampling distribution also applies to proportions for binary outcomes like success/failure.
g.text: For binary outcomes (success/failure), sample proportion p̂ follows normal distribution when:

g.data-narration: The inference procedure: take a sample, calculate statistic, use sampling distribution properties.

g.text: Use CLT to make inference about population

g.data-narration: Key takeaway: The standard error quantifies precision of your estimate and decreases with larger samples.

g.text: of a STATISTIC across all samples
CLT: x̄ is normal for n ≥ 30

g.data-narration: Computer scientists apply sampling distributions in A/B testing, algorithm analysis, and quality assurance.
This bridges probability theory to inferential statistics, enabling confidence intervals and hypothesis testing.
g.text: Sampling distributions connect sample statistics to population parameters.
This theory enables confidence intervals and hypothesis testing.

g.data-narration: Congratulations! You now understand the foundation of statistical inference - the sampling distribution.
g.text: You now understand sampling distributions - the foundation of statistical inference!
